overview: WAN Architecture covers how sites connect over provider networks — Metro Ethernet (Layer 2), MPLS VPN (Layer 3), and Internet VPNs — plus the OSPF-over-MPLS design pattern that appears in enterprise WAN scenarios.
definitions: Metro Ethernet (MetroE): a Layer 2 service where the provider network behaves like one large switch; E-Line (point-to-point), E-LAN (full mesh), E-Tree (hub-and-spoke).
MPLS VPN: a Layer 3 service where the provider actually routes the customer's IP packets.
Site-to-Site VPN: a permanent tunnel (IPsec/GRE) between two sites.
Remote Access VPN: a client-to-gateway tunnel, typically over TLS/HTTPS.
core_concepts: In an MPLS VPN, the customer edge (CE) router forms its routing adjacency with the provider edge (PE) router — never directly with a CE at another site.
The provider redistributes the customer's OSPF routes across the MPLS core using MP-BGP.
A 'Super Backbone' design lets multiple customer OSPF areas be connected together through the MPLS VPN core.
protocol_operation: CE router peers with PE router using OSPF.
PE redistributes those OSPF routes into MP-BGP to cross the provider's MPLS core.
The remote PE redistributes back into OSPF toward the remote CE.
packet_flow: Branch CE router (OSPF) -> Provider PE router -> MP-BGP carries customer routes across MPLS core -> remote PE -> redistributed back into OSPF -> remote CE router -> destination site.
port_numbers: HTTPS (used for Remote Access VPN, TLS-based): TCP 443.

troubleshooting.symptom: An inbound ACL on the WAN-facing interface blocks OSPF Hello packets.
troubleshooting.cause: OSPF Hellos were not explicitly permitted in the ACL, so the adjacency never forms.
troubleshooting.fix: Add a permit rule for the OSPF Hello traffic (e.g. permit ip host <neighbor> host 224.0.0.5) or otherwise allow OSPF multicast/unicast Hellos through the ACL.

best_practices: Choose MetroE (Layer 2) when the enterprise wants to run its own routing across sites; choose MPLS VPN (Layer 3) when the provider should handle routing.
security_notes: Site-to-Site VPNs (IPsec/GRE) and Remote Access VPNs (TLS/HTTPS) both encrypt traffic across otherwise-untrusted transport, unlike plain MetroE/MPLS which rely on the provider's own separation.
interview_questions: Why does a CE router form an OSPF adjacency with the PE, not with a CE at another site?
What is a Super Backbone design used for?
exam_traps: Assuming a branch CE directly peers with a remote-site CE over MPLS VPN — it always peers with its local PE.
enterprise_notes: The classic branch-office 'can't reach central server' scenario chains OSPF (over MPLS), NAT (Overload) and ACL together and must be diagnosed in that order.
